---
title: "Can a Quick Metabolism Test Predict Which Oyster Families Survive?"
subtitle: "Using a simple dye assay to forecast heat-stress hardiness"
author: "Steven Roberts"
date: today
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: simple
    slide-number: true
    embed-resources: true
    transition: none
    fig-align: center
---

## The problem

Oyster growers and breeders want to know **which family lines survive heat and
stress** — so they can farm the tough ones.

- Finding out the usual way means **heat-stressing hundreds of oysters until
  many die**.
- That's slow, expensive, and kills the animals.
- **Is there a faster, non-lethal shortcut?**

## A possible shortcut: a metabolism test

**Resazurin** is a blue dye that turns pink as living cells burn energy.

- Put oysters in the dye, watch the color change over a few hours.
- The speed and shape of that change = a readout of the animal's
  **metabolism** — how hard its cells are working.
- No animals are harmed.

::: {.fragment}
**Question:** does an oyster family's metabolism pattern reveal how well it will
survive stress?
:::

## What we compared

We had two independent sets of results on the **same 9 oyster families**:

::: {.incremental}
- **Survival record** — how each family fared across 7 heat and salinity
  experiments, boiled down to a single **hardiness score** (higher = tougher).
- **Metabolism fingerprint** — ~20 features describing each family's dye-color
  curve: how high its metabolism peaks, how fast it rises, how much it drops
  off under stress.
:::

## How we tested it

1. Summarize each family's metabolism curve into simple numbers.
2. Check which metabolism features line up with the hardiness score.
3. **The honest test:** hide one family, predict its hardiness from the other
   eight, then reveal the truth — and repeat for every family.

::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-note}
Only 9 families, so this is a promising **screening tool**, not a crystal ball.
:::
:::

## The surprise: tough families "stay calm"

The clearest pattern was the **opposite** of what you might guess.

- Families whose metabolism **spikes the most** when heated tend to **survive
  the least**.
- The **hardy** families keep their metabolism **restrained** under heat —
  they don't overreact.

## Tough families also crash later

![](../outputs/04-resazurin-family-phenotype-prediction/figures/best_single_predictor_scatter.png){height="430"}

The single best clue: hardy families take **longer** before their metabolism
takes its sharpest downturn. Each dot is a family.

## Putting the clues together

![](../outputs/04-resazurin-family-phenotype-prediction/figures/resazurin_index_scatter.png){height="430"}

Combining the top metabolism clues into one score **predicts the survival
ranking well** — the toughest family (5) and the most fragile (7) land at
opposite ends.

## What a "survivor" looks like on the test

::: {.incremental}
- **Restrained** metabolism under heat — not a big spike.
- A **slower, later** decline instead of an early crash.
- The signal is strongest when the test is run **during heat stress**, not at
  rest.
:::

## Bottom line

- A **cheap, non-lethal dye test** can flag which oyster families are likely
  **tough vs. fragile** — before any survival trial.
- Counterintuitively, **calm-and-steady** metabolism beats a big energy burst.
- Best used to **shortlist** promising families for confirmation, not to
  replace survival testing.

## What's next

- Test **more families** — the prediction gets sharper as the roster grows.
- Follow the **same animals** through both the dye test and survival to sharpen
  predictions down to the individual oyster.
- Fold results into breeding decisions for **heat-resilient stock**.
